era_context: Alexander the Great, now firmly established as king of Macedon and hegemon of Greece, launches his invasion of the Persian Achaemenid Empire. This year marks the beginning of one of history's most consequential military campaigns, which will result in the conquest of the Persian Empire and the spread of Hellenistic culture across the Mediterranean and Near East. The Persian Empire, though still vast and powerful, is weakened by internal instability following the death of Artaxerxes III Ochus. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (contemporary but no synchronization with Old World chronology)

events.title: Alexander invades Asia Minor; Battle of the Granicus River
events.description: Alexander crosses the Hellespont (Dardanelles Strait) with an army of approximately 35,000 infantry and 5,000 cavalry, beginning his invasion of the Persian Achaemenid Empire. He lands in northwestern Anatolia (ancient Phrygia) and immediately encounters a Persian force commanded by satraps (provincial governors) of the region. At the Granicus River (modern Biga River), Alexander defeats the Persian cavalry in a decisive battle, killing approximately 1,000 Persian soldiers while suffering only about 110 casualties. This victory opens the way for Alexander to advance into Anatolia and demonstrates the superiority of Macedonian tactics and discipline over Persian forces. The victory also boosts Alexander's prestige and attracts Greek mercenaries and allies to his cause.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, not present at this battle)

sources.name: Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Granicus River is one of the most famous battles of antiquity and is attested by multiple ancient sources. The date of 334 BCE is well-established in ancient chronologies. However, the exact casualty figures and some tactical details are debated among modern scholars.

events.title: Alexander advances through Anatolia; siege of Miletus
events.region: Anatolia (Ionia, western coast of modern Turkey)
events.description: Following his victory at the Granicus River, Alexander advances southward through Anatolia, liberating Greek cities from Persian rule. He besieges and captures the city of Miletus, an important Greek city on the Ionian coast that had been under Persian control. The siege demonstrates Alexander's ability to conduct siege warfare and his use of naval support. The capture of Miletus and other coastal cities begins to undermine Persian naval power in the Aegean Sea, which had been a major advantage for Persia.
events.certainty_note: The siege of Miletus is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 334 BCE. The exact duration and details of the siege are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Siege of Halicarnassus
events.region: Anatolia (Caria, southwestern coast of modern Turkey)
events.description: Alexander continues his advance southward along the Anatolian coast and besieges the city of Halicarnassus, the capital of the Persian satrapy of Caria. The city is defended by Memnon of Rhodes, a capable Persian general, and a strong garrison. The siege is lengthy and difficult, lasting several months. Alexander eventually captures the city through a combination of siege warfare and assault, though Memnon and some of the garrison escape by sea. The capture of Halicarnassus further consolidates Alexander's control of the Anatolian coast and eliminates a major Persian stronghold.
key_figures: Ada of Caria (Persian satrap, later allied with Alexander)
events.certainty_note: The siege of Halicarnassus is well-attested in ancient sources and is dated to 334 BCE (continuing into 333 BCE). The exact duration and details are somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Darius III Codomannus becomes Persian Great King
events.description: Darius III Codomannus becomes the Great King of Persia following the death of Artaxerxes III Ochus in 338 BCE and a period of instability. Darius III is a capable military commander but faces challenges in consolidating his power and dealing with rebellions in Egypt and the Levant. He is aware of Alexander's invasion of Anatolia but does not immediately respond with his full military force. Darius III will eventually face Alexander in battle at Issus in 333 BCE and at Gaugamela in 331 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Darius III's reign is well-attested in ancient sources. He became Great King around 336 BCE and ruled until his death in 330 BCE. The exact date of his accession is somewhat uncertain, but he was definitely ruling in 334 BCE.

events.title: Alexander visits the Oracle of Apollo at Didyma
events.region: Anatolia (Ionia, western coast of modern Turkey)
events.description: During his campaign in Anatolia, Alexander visits the Oracle of Apollo at Didyma (near Miletus), one of the most important oracles in the Greek world. The oracle, which had been silent for many years, is said to have spoken to Alexander, pronouncing him invincible. This event is significant for Alexander's propaganda and for his self-presentation as a divinely favored leader. The oracle's pronouncement boosts the morale of his troops and enhances his prestige among the Greek cities he is liberating from Persian rule.
key_figures: The priestess of Apollo at Didyma
events.certainty_note: The visit to Didyma is attested in ancient sources, but the exact date and the oracle's pronouncement are somewhat uncertain. The event may have been embellished in later accounts for propaganda purposes.

events.title: Aristotle's Lyceum continues in Athens under Theophrastus
events.description: In Athens, the philosophical school founded by Aristotle, the Lyceum, continues to operate. Aristotle himself is absent, having left Athens after the death of Alexander's father Philip II (due to anti-Macedonian sentiment in Athens). The school is being managed by Theophrastus, one of Aristotle's most accomplished students. The Lyceum is a center of learning and philosophical inquiry, covering subjects such as logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics, natural philosophy, and biology. The school will continue to be an important intellectual center for centuries.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The existence of the Lyceum in 334 BCE is well-established, but the exact details of its operation and Theophrastus's role are somewhat uncertain. Aristotle left Athens around 323 BCE, so he was not present in 334 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Granicus River is sometimes portrayed as a decisive victory that immediately opened the way for Alexander to conquer the Persian Empire. However, modern scholars note that the battle was primarily a cavalry engagement and that Persian resistance continued for several more years. The casualty figures reported in ancient sources (1,000 Persian soldiers killed vs. 110 Macedonian casualties) are likely exaggerated. The oracle at Didyma is said to have pronounced Alexander invincible, but this account may be a later embellishment designed to enhance Alexander's prestige. Some modern scholars question whether the oracle actually spoke to Alexander or whether this is a fabrication by later historians.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 334 BCE is dominated by Greek and Macedonian sources, particularly the accounts of Alexander's campaigns by Arrian, Plutarch, and Diodorus Siculus. These sources are based on earlier histories that are now lost, and they were written centuries after the events they describe. The perspective is decidedly pro-Alexander and Macedonian-centric. Persian sources are largely lost, and our knowledge of the Persian Empire in this period comes primarily from Greek sources, which are often hostile or biased. The accounts of Alexander's campaigns are often written in a heroic or propagandistic style, emphasizing Alexander's military genius and divine favor. Archaeological evidence from Anatolia provides some corroboration for the locations of battles and sieges, but does not resolve all uncertainties about the exact chronology and details of events.

graph_edges.note: Victory at the Granicus River allowed Alexander to advance through Anatolia
Capture of Miletus was followed by the siege of Halicarnassus
The Granicus victory set the stage for the Battle of Issus
Darius III's rule led to his confrontation with Alexander at Issus
The oracle at Didyma was visited during Alexander's Anatolian campaign
